content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 24,
1993
Joy Janes, Director
L.A. List
P.O.
Box 461280
Los Angeles,
CA
90046
RE:
Your Request For Advice
Our File No.
1-93-32
Dear Ms. Janes:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).l/
Because the facts presented in your letter do not
provide enough information to enable us to give you a specific
response to your question,
we can provide only the following
informal assistance and a general overview of the Act’s
requirements. 2 /
OUESTION
Will subscription fees received and payments made for the
purpose of developing lists of selected candidates running in
selected races and providing the lists to subscribers trigger a
reporting requirement for L.A. List?
CONCLUSION
Payments for producing a list of selected candidates running
in selected races constitute “expenditures” under the Act and may
trigger reporting obligations.
FACTS
L.A. List is a political organization formed to assist women
candidates running for non—partisan offices in Los Angeles County.
A board of directors will determine the candidate selection
1/ Government Code Section 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Joy Janes
Our File No.
1—93—32
Page
2
criteria and select the races.
A candidate review committee will
screen and interview the candidates to be recommended to persons
who have subscribed to the services provided by L.A. List.
L.A.
List will mail profiles of selected candidates running for
different offices to its subscribers.
Subscribers will decide
which candidates they will contribute to.
Contribution checks
will be made payable to the candidates’ campaign committees.
The
contribution checks will be mailed or delivered to L.A. List which
will tally,
bundle and forward the checks to the candidates’
committees.
None of the money collected by L.A. List from its
subscribers will be used to make direct contributions to the
candidates selected.
ANALYSIS
Because L.A. List is making expenditures to support selected
candidates, it may qualify as a committee under the Act pursuant
to Section 82013(a),
(b)
or (c).
The following analysis is
provided to give you guidance in determining the type of committee
L.A. List may become.
The campaign provisions of the Act require that committees
file campaign disclosure statements at specified times and places.
(Section 84200.)
Section 82013 provides that a “committee” is any
person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly does
any of the following:
(a)
Receives contributions totaling $1,000 or
more in a calendar year.
(b)
Makes independent expenditures totaling
$1,000 or more in a calendar year; or
(c)
Makes contributions totaling $10,000 or
more in a calendar year to or at the behest of
candidates or committees.
To qualify as a “recipient committee” under Section 82013(a),
L.A. List must receive contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a
calendar year.
Section 82015 defines “contribution”,
in pertinent part,
as:
[Aj payment,
a forgiveness of a loan,
a payment
of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise


Joy Janes
Our File No.
1—93—32
Page
3
to make a payment except to the extent that full
and adequate consideration is received unless it is
clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is
not made for political purposes.
An expenditure made
at the behest of a candidate,
committee or elected
officer is a contribution to the candidate,
committee
or elected officer unless full and adequate consideration
is received for making the expenditures.
Regulation 18215 defines a payment for “political purposes,”
in relevant part,
as a payment:
(1)
For the purpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the action of the voters for or against the
nomination or election of a candidate or candidates,
or the qualification or passage of any measure.
“Made at the behest” of a candidate means the payment was
made. under the control or at the direction of,
in cooperation,
consultation,
coordination, or concert with,
or at the request or
suggestion of the candidate or his or her controlled committee.
(Regulation 18215(b).)
L.A. List charges a $25 subscription fee for persons
interested in receiving the candidate lists.
Part of the $25
subscription fee is used to prepare the candidate lists and to
mail the lists to subscribers.
Based on the facts presented in
your letter,
we cannot determine if the subscribers are receiving
full and adequate consideration for the $25.
In addition,
we
cannot determine whether L.A. List’s expenditures are being made
at the behest of the candidates selected.
If full and adequate consideration is being given to the
subscribers,
L.A. List would not qualify as a recipient committee
pursuant to Section 82013(a).
However, if L.A. List makes
expenditures at the behest of the candidates, the expenditures
will be deemed contributions to the candidates and L.A. List may
qualify as a “major donor” committee pursuant to Section 82013(c).
If the expenditures are not made at the behest of the candidates,
then the expenditures may be “independent expenditures” and L.A.
List may qualify as an “independent expenditure” committee
pursuant to 82013(b).
The Davis Advice Letter,
No.
1-90—173,
copy
enclosed, provides an analysis of the primary differences between
“contributions” and “independent expenditures.”


Joy Janes
Our File No.
1—93—32
Page
4
To qualify as an “independent expenditure” committee pursuant
to Section 82013(b),
L.A. List must make expenditures totaling
$1,000 or more in a calendar year in connection with
communications which expressly advocate the election or defeat of
a clearly identified candidate(s)
or the qualification, passage or
defeat of a clearly identified measure(s), or taken as a whole and
in context,
unambiguously urges a particular result in an election
but which are not made to or at the behest of the affected
candidate(s)
or committee(s).
(Section 82031.)
To qualify as a “major donor” committee pursuant to Section
82013(c),
L.A. List must make expenditures to or at the behest of
candidates or committees totaling $10,000 or more in a calendar
year.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at 916/322—5662.
Sincerel
Mary
Kvasager
Political Reform Consultant
Enclosure

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Will subscription fees received and payments made for the
purpose of developing lists of selected candidates running in
selected races and providing the lists to subscribers trigger a
reporting requirement for L.A. List?

CONCLUSION: Payments for producing a list of selected candidates running
in selected races constitute “expenditures” under the Act and may
trigger reporting obligations.
